name: Classical and Conventional Phillips-Type Curves, 1982-2010

book_quotes.quote: The contrast between the classical and conventional Phillips curves becomes even starker when we look separately at the pre- and post-neoliberal eras of 1948-1981 and 1982-2010, respectively.
Figure 15.9 Classical and Conventional Phillip-Type Curves, 1982-2010

weakening of labor and of the welfare state lowered the intercept of the wage-share Phillips curve and made its slope less steep (figure 14.14). From an econometric point of view, this would suggest the use of both intercept and slope dummies for the entire second period. Econometric estimations by Handfas (2012) are discussed in the next section.

primary_source.publication: Same as S1505/S1506 (Inflation pi from BEA NIPA T1.1.4; sigma from BEA T5.3.5 / T1.16; uL from BLS)
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Subset of S1505 series, restricted to years 1982-2010 (post-Volcker / neoliberal era)

formula: Sub-period restriction of S1505 to 1982-2010. Variables: pi, (1 - sigma'), uL.

components.label: Inflation rate (pi), 1982-2010
components.source: BEA NIPA T1.1.4 % change in GDP deflator

components.label: Growth-utilization rate (sigma), 1982-2010
components.source: BEA NIPA T5.3.5 line 2 / T1.16 line 2 (Handfas 2012)

extension_candidates.subseries_label: BEA + BLS modern series, post-1982 extension to present
concerns: Post-1982 segment is the natural extension target since regime is consistent.

methodology_notes: Post-neoliberal sub-period of S1505 — same data, restricted to 1982-2010.
Shaikh links the regime shift to the Reagan/Volcker era 'weakening of labor and of the welfare state' (p.709).
Handfas (2012) econometric tests rely on this sub-period dummy structure.
open_questions: Whether 1982 (Volcker shock disinflation) or 1981 (Reagan inauguration) is the proper regime-break year — Shaikh uses 1982.

review_history.notes: CD2 S085 ported; quotes verified against PDF pp.709-710 and Appendix 15.1 p.896
